scenes.title: Phishing, Rug Pulls & Smart Contract Exploits

elements.text: Threats: Phishing, Rug Pulls & Smart Contract Exploits
Phishing Attack Types

Wallet drainer sites
Fake MetaMask / Trust Wallet pages request
your seed phrase. Never enter it on any site.

Malicious token approvals
Signing 'approve' grants a contract unlimited
access to your tokens. Always check what you sign.

Fake support DMs
'Binance Support' DMing you first = scam.
No legit team asks for your keys or seed.

Address poisoning
Scammer sends dust to wallet with similar
first/last chars. You copy wrong address.
Rug Pull Red Flags

A rug pull = devs drain liquidity and disappear

6 red flags before investing:

⚠ Anonymous team with no track record
⚠ No audit from a reputable firm
⚠ Top 10 wallets hold >50% of supply
⚠ Liquidity not locked (check DexTools)
⚠ Unrealistic APY promises (1000%+)
⚠ Contract has mint function — devs can
   create infinite new tokens

Always check token contract on Etherscan
Smart Contract Vulnerabilities

Reentrancy — attacker calls contract repeatedly before balance updates (The DAO hack, $60M lost)
Integer overflow — arithmetic wraps around: 255 + 1 = 0 (Solidity before 0.8 was vulnerable)
Access control — missing permission checks let anyone call admin functions
Oracle manipulation — flash loans distort price feeds to trick lending protocols

How to stay safe: only use protocols audited by CertiK, Trail of Bits, or OpenZeppelin
Time in market + track record are your best proxies for smart contract safety

actions.text: Security at the High School level means understanding the actual mechanics of how attacks work — not just 'be careful.' Phishing in crypto goes beyond fake emails: wallet drainer sites, malicious token approvals, fake support messages, and address poisoning are the four main vectors targeting intermediate users.

Rug pulls happen when developers drain liquidity and disappear. Six red flags tell you to stay away: anonymous team, no audit, concentrated supply, unlocked liquidity, unrealistic APYs, and a mint function in the contract. Always check the token contract on Etherscan before investing in any new project.

Smart contract vulnerabilities caused the biggest DeFi hacks in history. Reentrancy, integer overflow, access control gaps, and oracle manipulation are the four major categories. Your protection is simple: only use protocols audited by reputable firms, and give new protocols time to prove themselves before trusting large amounts.

elements.text: OPSEC, Transaction Hygiene & Account Security
Operational Security (OPSEC)

Online behavior that protects your holdings:

• Never reveal how much crypto you hold publicly
• Don't flex wins on social media — SIM swap
  attackers look for high-value targets
• Use a dedicated email for crypto accounts
• Use a password manager (Bitwarden, 1Password)
• Enable hardware 2FA (YubiKey) on exchanges
• Use a VPN when accessing wallets on public WiFi
• Never discuss holdings on Discord or Telegram
• Separate 'hot' (trading) from 'cold' (savings) funds
Transaction Verification Checklist

Before every transaction:

✓ Verify address manually — first 4 AND last 4 chars
✓ Send a test transaction for large amounts
✓ Check the network (mainnet vs testnet)
✓ Verify you have enough native token for gas
✓ For DeFi: read the approval — what contract,
  what tokens, what spending limit?
✓ Check gas fees aren't abnormally high
  (front-run indicator)
✓ Use revoke.cash monthly to audit
  token approvals and revoke unused ones
SIM Swap Attacks

Attacker calls your carrier, impersonates you, and ports your phone number to their SIM card.
Now they receive all your SMS 2FA codes.

How it happens: social engineering + leaked
personal data (name, last 4 SSN, account PIN)

Protection:
• Never use SMS 2FA for crypto — use authenticator app
• Enable carrier account PIN + port freeze
• Use hardware key (YubiKey) as 2FA where possible
Intermediate Security Checklist

✓ Hardware wallet for >$1,000 in holdings
✓ Seed phrase stored offline, never digital
✓ Authenticator app 2FA (not SMS) everywhere
✓ Dedicated crypto email with strong password
✓ Monthly token approval audit (revoke.cash)
✓ Never click links from Discord/Telegram DMs
✓ Test transaction before sending large amounts
✓ VPN on public networks when accessing wallets

actions.text: OPSEC — operational security — is about the habits that protect you before an attack happens. The biggest one: never publicly reveal how much crypto you hold. SIM swap attackers specifically target people who flex crypto wins on social media. Use a dedicated email for crypto, a password manager, and authenticator app 2FA — never SMS.

Transaction hygiene saves money. Before every send: verify the first four and last four characters of the address manually, send a small test amount first, and always read DeFi approvals carefully. Check what contract you're approving, what tokens it can access, and the spending limit. Use revoke.cash monthly to clean up old approvals.

SIM swap is one of the most dangerous attacks because it bypasses SMS two-factor authentication entirely. The attacker impersonates you with your carrier and ports your number to their SIM. The fix: switch every crypto account to an authenticator app, enable a carrier account PIN, and use a hardware key like YubiKey where possible.

Your intermediate security checklist covers eight habits: hardware wallet for significant holdings, offline seed phrase storage, authenticator-based 2FA, dedicated crypto email, monthly approval audits, never clicking unsolicited links, test transactions, and VPN on public networks. Implement all eight and you're in the top ten percent of crypto users for security practices.

scenes.title: Exchange Safety, CEX vs DEX Risk & Recovery Planning

elements.text: Exchange Safety, CEX vs DEX Risk & Recovery
CEX Risk — Centralized Exchange Safety

FTX collapsed Nov 2022 — $8B in customer funds lost
Gox collapsed 2014 — 850,000 BTC lost

CEX safety checklist:
✓ Use regulated, audited exchanges (Coinbase, Kraken)
✓ Only keep trading amounts on exchange
✓ Withdraw profits to self-custody regularly
✓ Check Proof of Reserves (does exchange publish?)
✓ Separate exchange account email from personal
✓ Never share 2FA codes with 'support'
✓ Monitor for withdrawal whitelist options
DEX Risk — Decentralized Exchange Safety

DEX: non-custodial — you keep your keys
No registration, trade any token

DEX risks:
• Smart contract bugs (no insurance)
• Front-running bots (MEV) — sandwich attacks
  inflate your buy price and dump after your tx
• Slippage — price moves between sign and confirm
• Fake token contracts (same name, different address)

Safety: use official frontend URLs, set max
slippage <1% for stablecoins, <2% for majors,
verify contract address before any new token trade
Recovery Planning

What if something goes wrong?

Create a crypto will:
• Secure location of seed phrases and hardware wallets
• List of exchanges, wallets, and approx holdings
• Instructions for next-of-kin (lawyer or trusted person)

If you're compromised:
1. Move remaining funds to new wallet immediately
2. Revoke all token approvals from compromised wallet
3. Report to exchange if CEX was breached
4. Document everything for tax/insurance
Security is Your Edge

Most traders lose money to markets.
The second-biggest loss category is security failures.

OnlyCrypto's copy trading model reduces risk:
• Trading through the platform — no private key exposure
• Expert traders manage the position
• You maintain control of entry and exit

Security knowledge is protective alpha.
Every attack you understand is one you avoid.
The goal is to keep what you earn.

actions.text: Centralized exchanges carry counterparty risk — FTX lost eight billion dollars of customer funds in 2022, and Mt. Gox lost 850,000 Bitcoin before that. The rule is simple: only keep on exchange what you're actively trading. Withdraw profits to self-custody regularly, and only use exchanges that publish proof of reserves.

DEXs give you full custody but introduce different risks. MEV bots run sandwich attacks — they see your pending transaction, buy before you, and sell right after, inflating your price. Slippage can also hurt you if price moves between signing and confirmation. Always verify the contract address before trading any new token — fake token contracts with identical names are a common trap.

Recovery planning is something most crypto holders skip until it's too late. Create a crypto will — a secure document with seed phrase locations, exchange accounts, and approximate holdings. If you're ever compromised, act immediately: move remaining funds to a new wallet, revoke all token approvals, and document everything. Speed is everything when your wallet is at risk.

Security knowledge is protective alpha — every attack you understand is one you avoid. Markets take money. Security failures also take money. OnlyCrypto's model reduces your attack surface because you trade through the platform without exposing private keys directly. The goal of everything in this module is simple: keep what you earn.

questions.question: What is an address poisoning attack?
options: Sending malware to your email address
A scammer hacking your exchange account via your email
A scammer sending dust to your wallet from an address with similar first and last characters so you copy the wrong address
Injecting malicious code into a smart contract's address field
questions.answer: A scammer sending dust to your wallet from an address with similar first and last characters so you copy the wrong address
questions.analysis: In address poisoning, scammers send tiny amounts of crypto from a wallet address that looks nearly identical to one you've interacted with before — matching the first 4 and last 4 characters. When you copy an address from your transaction history, you accidentally copy theirs. Always verify the full address, not just the ends.

questions.question: Why is SMS two-factor authentication considered weak for crypto accounts?
options: SMS codes expire too quickly
Carriers don't support SMS 2FA for crypto platforms
SIM swap attacks allow attackers to port your phone number and receive your SMS codes
SMS 2FA only works on Android devices
questions.answer: SIM swap attacks allow attackers to port your phone number and receive your SMS codes
questions.analysis: SIM swap attacks involve an attacker social-engineering your phone carrier into transferring your number to their SIM card. Once they have your number, they receive all SMS codes sent to it — bypassing SMS 2FA entirely. Use authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) or hardware keys instead.

questions.question: What is a reentrancy attack in smart contracts?
options: When the same user logs in from two locations simultaneously
An attacker repeatedly calling a contract to drain funds before the balance updates
When a contract runs out of gas mid-execution
A bot that enters and exits a liquidity pool in the same block
questions.answer: An attacker repeatedly calling a contract to drain funds before the balance updates
questions.analysis: Reentrancy exploits a contract that sends funds before updating its internal balance. The attacker's malicious contract receives funds, then immediately calls back into the victim contract before the balance is updated — draining it repeatedly. The DAO hack in 2016 used this exact exploit to steal $60M in ETH.

questions.question: What does a 'mint function' in a token's smart contract indicate as a red flag?
options: The contract can generate NFTs on demand
The contract allows the developer to create unlimited new tokens at any time, potentially destroying your holdings' value
The token requires GPU mining to produce new coins
The contract automatically mints tokens for liquidity providers
questions.answer: The contract allows the developer to create unlimited new tokens at any time, potentially destroying your holdings' value
questions.analysis: An unrestricted mint function gives the contract owner the ability to create new tokens at will. In a rug pull scenario, devs can mint millions of tokens and dump them on the market, collapsing the price. Legitimate tokens either have no mint function, a fixed supply, or governance-controlled minting.

questions.question: What is a MEV sandwich attack on a DEX?
options: A bot that splits your order into smaller pieces to reduce slippage
A bot that front-runs your buy order and back-runs it to profit from your price impact
Two liquidity providers competing for the same pool
A validator that reorders transactions to earn more fees
questions.answer: A bot that front-runs your buy order and back-runs it to profit from your price impact
questions.analysis: MEV (Maximal Extractable Value) sandwich attacks work in three steps: the bot sees your pending buy transaction, inserts its own buy before yours (front-run), lets your transaction execute (which moves the price), then immediately sells after yours (back-run). You buy at a higher price, the bot profits from the difference. Setting lower slippage tolerance limits how much they can extract.

actions.text: Security quiz time. These questions test the mechanics of real attacks — address poisoning, SIM swaps, reentrancy, mint functions, and MEV sandwich attacks. Understanding how each attack works is the only way to reliably protect against it.

You've completed the Security module at High School level. You now understand the attack vectors that take money from intermediate crypto users — and more importantly, the specific habits and tools that block them. Security knowledge is permanent — these defenses apply regardless of which protocols or exchanges you use.
